title: The Repressor of Over Much Blaming of the Clergy
summary: The checked-in ahb1325 XML is an exact repository artifact at pinned CME commit e1d77c8, representing University of Michigan item AHB1325.0001.001. U-M describes that 2006 digital item as uncorrected OCR made from and linked to 822 distinct 600 dpi TIFF page images of Churchill Babington's two-volume 1860 first edition. The U-M image sequence aggregates both volumes and places their title pages together before one merged stream; the encoded target is Pecock's prologue and five parts, nodes :3–:8 and printed pp. 1–565, while front matter and the edition's separate back-matter works remain omitted-page milestones. Babington states that he printed the Repressor directly from Cambridge University Library MS Kk.4.26, whose 190 numbered vellum leaves carry the work from fol. 1 through the final numbered leaf; the 1858 catalog also excludes one blank leaf at each end. Modern Cambridge cataloging calls Kk.4.26 the only extant manuscript and dates its production c. 1449–57, while the work itself is conventionally dated c. 1449. No complete public manuscript facsimile was verified on 2026-07-16, so the practical next route is a whole-codex-first Cambridge imaging enquiry rather than treating the two complete edition scans as manuscript facsimiles.

entities.label: General Corpus source XML for ahb1325
entities.description: The exact checked-in CME XML artifact. Its cataloged work is nodes AHB1325.0001.001:3–:8; nodes :1–:2 and :9 are edition front/back-matter carriers outside the work boundary.
notes: The XML identifier is AHB1325.0001.001; no fuzzy alias is required.
The U-M header reads 'Recock, Reginald'; this is preserved as a source-record error/variant and not propagated as the normalized author name.

entities.label: University of Michigan CME item AHB1325.0001.001
alternate_labels: The repressor of over much blaming of the clergy
entities.description: University of Michigan's 2006 Level-1 TEI-in-Libraries electronic item. Its own EDITORIALDECL says it was produced by OCR, left uncorrected, and not content-edited. It encodes Babington pp. 1–565 as text while retaining front/back matter mainly as omitted-page milestones.

identifiers.scheme: Library of Congress call number in U-M header

entities.reproduction_format: Uncorrected OCR with Level-1 TEI-in-Libraries encoding and linked page milestones
notes: The encoding's language header says eng; the medieval work is normalized as Middle English, while Babington's English/Latin apparatus remains an editorial layer.
The item-specific uncorrected-OCR statement controls over generic collection descriptions of production practice.

entities.label: U-M page-image sequence for AHB1325.0001.001
entities.description: The historical U-M delivery layer recorded in the XML as 822 distinct linked TIFF images at 600 dpi. The sequence aggregates the two 1860 volumes, places the two volume title pages at sequences 11 and 13, carries the target printed pp. 1–565 at sequences 111–675, and continues into edition back matter through sequence 822.
entities.reproduction_format: 822 linked 600 dpi TIFF G4 page images, each with a unique REF/SEQ in the source XML
notes: Historical XML proves that the image production layer existed; it does not establish a currently downloadable complete TIFF bundle.
The scanned physical copies and any relation between them and the two independently cached Internet Archive carriers remain unidentified.

entities.label: Babington's 1860 two-volume edition of The Repressor
entities.description: The conceptual two-volume first print edited directly from CUL MS Kk.4.26. Pecock's Repressor occupies pp. 1–565; the edition also includes a historical introduction, content summary, textual apparatus, and separate back-matter works and reference matter.

bibliographic.citation: Reginald Pecock, The Repressor of Over Much Blaming of the Clergy, ed. Churchill Babington, 2 vols., Rerum Britannicarum Medii Aevi Scriptores 19 (London: Longman, Green, Longman, and Roberts, 1860).

bibliographic.series: Rerum Britannicarum Medii Aevi Scriptores; Chronicles and Memorials of Great Britain and Ireland during the Middle Ages, no. 19
bibliographic.pages: target work pp. 1–565 across two volumes

notes: This conceptual edition is distinct from each bibliographic volume and from every scan carrier.
Babington's statement that the work is 'now first printed' from Kk.4.26 supports direct transcription and first publication.

entities.label: Cambridge University Library MS Kk.4.26
entities.description: The sole extant Repressor manuscript according to Binski and Zutshi's Cambridge catalog. The 1858 catalog describes a small vellum folio of 190 numbered leaves, excluding one blank leaf at each end, written in double columns of about 37 lines in a fifteenth-century hand. The target begins on fol. 1 and ends on the final numbered leaf; the exact terminal side is not stated in the checked catalog evidence.

date_statements.label: manuscript production
date_statements.notes: This is the modern catalog's manuscript-production date, distinct from the conventional c. 1449 composition date for the work.

date_statements.label: later exhibition note
date_statements.notes: The later note records exhibition before a lord in his chapel at Lambeth; its historical interpretation and the exact legal setting remain debated.

holding.notes: Current CUL guidance routes many medieval two-letter shelfmarks through the nineteenth-century printed catalog rather than an exact modern online item record.

physical_description.extent: 190 numbered leaves; the 1858 catalog additionally excludes one blank leaf at each end
physical_description.format: small folio; double columns; about 37 lines per column
notes: Initials are illuminated and capitals variously colored; several later hands occur.

The 1858 description therefore accounts for at least 192 leaves, but a current cover-to-cover physical inventory, binding description, stub count, and exact number of additional endleaves were not obtained.

Babington reports at least three early correction hands; Smith reports seeing two and describes their changes as non-substantive. Binski and Zutshi say the manuscript was probably corrected by the author. These remain attributed, non-collapsed claims.
The later title attributes the work to Bishop Pecock, while the author's name does not occur in the body according to the 1858 catalog.
Babington's direct-editorial statement, Pecock's self-references in other works, and later scholarship jointly support authorship; no claim of an autograph main hand is made.
The 1858 catalog's Archbishop Rotheram donation statement is preserved as an older claim; Babington later judged it probably erroneous.

entities.label: CUL manuscript catalog volume III, entry 2041
entities.description: The 1858 institutional catalog expression describing Kk. IV. 26 on pp. 673–674.

bibliographic.citation: A Catalogue of the Manuscripts Preserved in the Library of the University of Cambridge, vol. III (Cambridge: University Press, 1858), no. 2041, pp. 673–674.
bibliographic.title: A Catalogue of the Manuscripts Preserved in the Library of the University of Cambridge

entities.label: Internet Archive scan of CUL manuscript catalog volume III
entities.description: A complete 788-page Internet Archive PDF delivery of the 1858 catalog volume. It is a supporting catalog scan, not a facsimile of Kk.4.26 and contains no target work text.

entities.label: Binski and Zutshi's Western Illuminated Manuscripts
entities.description: The 2011 Cambridge University Press catalog expression. Its introduction and manuscript list identify Kk.4.26 as the only extant Repressor manuscript, probably corrected by Pecock, and date catalog no. 256 c. 1449–57.

bibliographic.citation: Paul Binski and Patrick Zutshi, with Stella Panayotova, Western Illuminated Manuscripts: A Catalogue of the Collection in Cambridge University Library (Cambridge: Cambridge University Press, 2011).
bibliographic.title: Western Illuminated Manuscripts: A Catalogue of the Collection in Cambridge University Library

entities.label: PagePlace preview PDF of Binski and Zutshi 2011
entities.description: A 73-page provider preview carrying the title matter, copyright statement, introduction, and manuscript list including no. 256. It is partial relative to the full catalog and contains no target work text or manuscript images of Kk.4.26.

entities.label: Smith's 2012 dissertation on Pecock and vernacular theology

bibliographic.citation: Jennifer Anh-Thư Tran Smith, Reginald Pecock and Vernacular Theology in Pre-Reformation England (PhD diss., University of California, Los Angeles, 2012).
bibliographic.title: Reginald Pecock and Vernacular Theology in Pre-Reformation England

entities.label: Kelly's 2021 study of the judicial processes against Pecock

bibliographic.citation: Henry Ansgar Kelly, ‘Judicial Processes for and against Bishop Reginald Pecock: New Perspectives on the Mechanisms of His Downfall,’ Viator 52.1 (2021): 359–430, doi:10.1484/J.VIATOR.5.130891.

It is practical access evidence, not part of the Repressor's textual lineage.

access.access_method: Contact CUL Special Collections to arrange consultation in the Special Collections Reading Room, subject to current reader and handling conditions.
Dated negative check of deterministic Cambridge Digital Library item, IIIF, and metadata routes plus broad CUDL and web searches; this is not proof that no institutional surrogate exists.
notes: The item-view route returned HTTP 403, while tested IIIF/metadata routes returned server or missing-key errors; no canvas inventory or complete public file was obtained.
An institutional enquiry could overturn this negative.

access.access_method: First ask whether CUL can supply an existing complete digital surrogate or complete microfilm. If none is available, request a full-color reproduction of the whole physical codex: front and back covers, boards and inner boards, spine, pastedowns, every endleaf and flyleaf including the cataloged blank leaf at each end, all stubs, every recto and verso of numbered fols. 1–190, edges if offered, and any other bound surfaces. Identify the target work separately as fols. 1–190, beginning fol. 1 and ending on the final numbered leaf, exact terminal side to be confirmed. Use a target-only copy only if CUL says whole-object capture is infeasible.
access.contact: CUL Imaging Services via the online order form
access.format: Provider-quoted electronic delivery; current guide describes high-resolution uncompressed JPEG, TIFF on request, and high-resolution JPEG for microfilm scans
notes: No enquiry or order has been submitted; availability, condition approval, existing-surrogate coverage, price, delivery format, and reuse terms remain unconfirmed.
Request private-study supply terms and any separate publication or redistribution permission independently.

editorial_practices.source_feature: A manuscript with numerous early corrections, some on erasures, plus later annotations
editorial_practices.representation: Original first-hand readings are reported in notes when recoverable; erased readings are not always or usually discoverable and are then unreported
editorial_practices.description: Babington distinguishes the principal text from multiple correction hands and records recoverable original readings, but explicitly acknowledges that erasure can prevent recovery. His suggestion that principal corrections are Pecock's own is an attribution, not a settled fact.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Manuscript contractions and ambiguous abbreviated forms, including signs usable for er/re and per/par/por
editorial_practices.representation: Expanded print forms chosen by the editor, with uncertainty and scribal inconsistency described in the introduction
editorial_practices.description: Babington expands manuscript abbreviation signs and discusses cases where the scribe's contractions do not determine one unique full spelling. The print should not be read as a facsimile of manuscript letterforms.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Two-volume scholarly edition with introduction, target work, appended distinct works, glossary, and index
editorial_practices.representation: OCR text for the Repressor on pp. 1–565; front and back matter largely represented by omitted DIV1 containers and page milestones
editorial_practices.description: The U-M item gives searchable OCR for the target work but not a homogeneous full transcription of every page represented in the 822-image sequence. Its OCR was explicitly left uncorrected and content-unedited.

evidence.summary: Identifies the 426-page volume II carrier, Princeton Theological Seminary contribution, New Jersey scanner, 2014 public date, and exact PDF derivative size/checksums; rights/license fields are absent.
Identifies volume I, 432 pages, University of Toronto contribution, 1860 publication, and the exact item-level statement 'Image: No Known Copyright.'

evidence.citation: Cambridge University Library, Archives, Modern and Medieval Manuscripts and University Archives
evidence.locator: consultation, searching collections, and Imaging Services sections
evidence.summary: Provides current CUL physical consultation, contact, printed-catalog discovery, and imaging-service routes. It is not an exact item catalog record for Kk.4.26.

evidence.citation: A Catalogue of the Manuscripts Preserved in the Library of the University of Cambridge, vol. III (1858), no. 2041
evidence.summary: Supports shelfmark, physical description, 190 numbered leaves plus a blank at each end, target start/end, later hands, names, 1457 note, and the older Rotheram gift claim.

evidence.citation: Paul Binski and Patrick Zutshi, with Stella Panayotova, Western Illuminated Manuscripts (Cambridge University Press, 2011)
evidence.locator: title/copyright pages, catalog methodology, introduction, and manuscript list no. 256
evidence.summary: Supports the modern catalog identity, only-extant-manuscript statement, probable authorial correction attribution, and c. 1449–57 manuscript-production date.

evidence.citation: Jennifer Anh-Thư Tran Smith, Reginald Pecock and Vernacular Theology in Pre-Reformation England (PhD diss., UCLA, 2012)
evidence.locator: front matter, chronology discussion, Appendix D pp. 299–301, and bibliography
evidence.summary: Supports the c. 1449 work date, dissertation copyright, and Smith's direct assessment of two correction hands, minor corrections, and Thomas Atkyns's annotations in Kk.4.26.

evidence.citation: Henry Ansgar Kelly, ‘Judicial Processes for and against Bishop Reginald Pecock,’ Viator 52.1 (2021): 359–430
evidence.summary: Supports ca. 1449 chronology, Kk.4.26 identity, probable Whittington beginning, and a cautious interpretation of the 1457 exhibition note.

evidence.citation: Dated search for a public complete digital surrogate of CUL MS Kk.4.26
evidence.locator: deterministic CUDL item/IIIF/metadata routes plus CUDL-domain and broad web searches
evidence.summary: No resolvable Presentation manifest, auditable canvas inventory, complete public PDF, or item-specific digital-content record was found. The view returned 403 and tested IIIF/metadata routes returned server or missing-key errors. This dated negative remains subject to institutional correction.

evidence.citation: Cambridge University Library Cultural Heritage Imaging Laboratory, Ordering Images
evidence.locator: ordering, bespoke/comprehensive imaging, online form, quotation, payment, delivery, and permissions guidance
evidence.summary: Supports a mediated imaging route subject to material condition, quote, payment, and terms; it does not establish that Kk.4.26 has been approved, priced, or already digitized.

evidence.citation: Cambridge University Library, Making Imaging and Permissions Requests at Cambridge University Library
evidence.summary: Explains classmark and folio-range request fields, asks staff to check any selected microfilm option, requires copyright declarations and terms, and describes electronic high-resolution JPEG/TIFF delivery and payment. It supports request planning only.
notes: qpdf's recoverable object-offset-zero warnings were retained without repair because every page extracted successfully and the provider bytes remained usable.

open_questions.question: Does the pinned CME repository's root Unlicense expressly cover the inherited U-M XML data, transcription, encoding, and markup at CME/source/CME_phase_1-2/ahb1325.xml?
next_steps: Seek a path-specific licensing or provenance statement from the CME maintainers.
Until then, retain unknown repository-artifact status and do not transfer the software-worded root license to upstream textual, edition, or image layers.

open_questions.question: Can U-M provide a stable complete download of the 822-image two-volume sequence, identify the scanned physical copies, and clarify exact OCR/XML versus image-layer rights?
next_steps: Ask U-M for the complete image inventory or bundle and production record.
Ask whether the current No Copyright–United States statement applies separately to OCR/XML and TIFF delivery despite the generic 2006 restriction warning.
Compare any supplied images with both independent Internet Archive carriers before asserting copy identity or derivation.

open_questions.question: Can Cambridge University Library supply an existing complete surrogate or, if none exists, a new full-color whole-physical-codex reproduction of Kk.4.26 with an auditable surface inventory and explicit reuse terms?
next_steps: Use the imaging form to ask first for an existing complete digital surrogate or complete microfilm.

If none exists, request covers, boards and inner boards, spine, pastedowns, all endleaves/flyleaves and blanks, stubs, every recto and verso of numbered fols. 1–190, edges if offered, and any other bound surfaces; identify fols. 1–190 separately as the work range.
Ask for the complete delivered image inventory and an explicit list of surfaces omitted for condition or handling reasons.
Ask separately for private-study supply, repository/Git redistribution, publication, and commercial/POD terms.
notes: No enquiry or order has yet been submitted; this is a planned mediated route, not pending external correspondence.

open_questions.question: What is the current complete physical extent of Kk.4.26, including binding, covers, pastedowns, all endleaves/flyleaves, blanks, stubs, foliation anomalies, and the exact recto/verso endpoint of the Repressor?
next_steps: Request a current codicological description or staff-supplied surface inventory together with any reproduction.
Do not convert the 1858 statement '190 leaves excluding a blank leaf at each end' into a claim that the complete current object has exactly 192 leaves or no other surfaces.
Confirm the exact terminal side before adding a fol. 190r/190v locator.

open_questions.question: Which correction hands in Kk.4.26 are contemporary, whether any is Pecock's, and whether the manuscript was an authorially supervised or trial-related copy?
next_steps: Compare the whole-codex images paleographically with authenticated or securely attributed Pecock material if available.

Reconcile Babington's at-least-three-hand account, Binski and Zutshi's 'probably corrected by the author,' and Smith's two-hand/non-substantive assessment without treating any one as certain by default.

open_questions.question: Did Archbishop Thomas Rotheram actually present Kk.4.26 to Cambridge University Library, or is the 1858 catalog attribution a later donor-book inference?
next_steps: Check the earliest CUL shelf lists, donor records, ownership inscriptions, and the full provenance discussion in current cataloging.
Retain the 1858 Rotheram claim and Babington's later rejection as separately attributed statements until primary acquisition evidence resolves them.

open_questions.question: What exact item-level image rights statement and provider terms govern the volume II scan carrier repressorofoverm02peco?
next_steps: Obtain an exact current Theological Commons or contributing-library item record for volume II and record its rights statement separately from volume I.
Do not transfer volume I's No Known Copyright label to the distinct volume II scan carrier.

review_notes: The primary historical path is repository artifact → U-M uncorrected OCR → U-M 822-image aggregate → Babington 1860 two-volume edition → CUL MS Kk.4.26. The two Internet Archive PDFs are independent volume-specific facsimile routes, not upstream OCR sources unless future production evidence proves that direction.

The target is one medieval prose work, nodes :3–:8 and printed pp. 1–565. Babington's appended Bury excerpts, Abbreviatio, Gascoigne extract, glossary, and index are outside the work boundary even though the U-M image carrier continues through them.

Volume I and volume II are separately modeled bibliographic entities, and each Internet Archive scan is a distinct carrier with its own contributor, production date, access record, local copies, and rights assessment.

The U-M sequence's 822 PB occurrences are 822 unique TIFF references. Target prose pages are sequences 111–675; sequence 110 is a blank inside the prologue container. Both volume title pages were moved into the front of the merged digital sequence.
All 48 XML L elements are Babington apparatus, not Pecock verse: 44 Latin lines in Part II notes and four Middle English lines in a Part III note. The work remains prose-only.

Kk.4.26 contains the target from fol. 1 through the final numbered leaf among 190 numbered leaves. The exact final side, present binding extent, and every non-numbered surface remain open rather than being inferred.

Babington's possible authorial-correction and provenance conclusions, Binski and Zutshi's probable-authorial-correction claim, and Smith's two-corrector/non-substantive assessment remain separately attributed.
Every cached source is recorded once under the entity it delivers. The cache is private/ignored; local presence does not authorize public redistribution.

Kelly's article and Smith's dissertation are provisionally typed catalog_description because schema 1.0.0 has no study entity type; their repository PDFs are separate facsimile/delivery entities and do not enter the textual derivation chain.

No Cambridge manuscript image request was submitted. The manifest records a whole-host-first request specification and a dated no-public-source finding, not pending correspondence, approval, price, delivery, or permission.
